description: The provided image appears to be a placeholder graphic ('Check for updates' badge) rather than the actual scientific figure content. Based on the accompanying paper metadata regarding anisotropic deformation of low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%), the intended figure likely presents experimental data comparing mechanical behavior along Rolling Direction (RD) versus Transverse Direction (TD). Potential content includes tensile stress-strain curves showing ultimate tensile strength ~864 MPa and total elongation ~69% for RD samples, strain hardening rate plots (2052 MPa for RD vs 1828 MPa for TD), or synchrotron HEXRD data tracking strain-induced martensitic transformation kinetics. Alternatively, the figure might display EBSD inverse pole figure maps revealing the banded microstructure of coarse δ-ferrite (25 vol.%, ~38 μm) and ultrafine γ+α aggregates (56 vol.%), or μ-DIC strain partitioning maps showing iso-strain conditions in RD versus strain concentration in δ-ferrite for TD. The figure supports the core conclusion that anisotropic hardening arises from directional differences in strain partitioning and martensite nucleation at phase boundaries, but specific visual details, subfigure labels, axes, and quantitative curve features remain unreadable from the placeholder image.

The figure presents a pseudo-binary equilibrium phase diagram for the Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-xAl (wt.%) steel system, calculated using Thermo-Calc software with the TCFE9 database. The vertical axis represents temperature ranging from 500 to 1600°C, while the horizontal axis shows aluminum content from 0 to 10 weight percent. The diagram delineates multiple phase stability regions including liquid (L), α+L, γ+L, single-phase austenite (γ), γ+α, γ+θ, γ+α+κ, and α+κ. A red star marked 'Current Study' indicates the specific composition of Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al (approximately 5 wt% Al) at the intercritical annealing temperature of 850°C, confirming that the selected processing conditions lie within the γ+α two-phase field. This thermodynamic calculation validates the experimental design for obtaining the desired microstructure containing ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates and δ-ferrite, as the annealing temperature positions the alloy in the two-phase austenite-ferrite region where partial phase transformation can occur.

This figure presents synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) characterization of a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) under uniaxial tensile deformation. Panel (a) illustrates the experimental setup at the P02.1 beamline of PETRA III (DESY), showing the 60 keV incident beam, slit system, dog-bone specimen (gauge dimensions 12 mm × 2 mm × 1.2 mm), beam stop, and area detector capturing Debye-Scherrer rings, with coordinate definitions for rolling direction (RD), transverse direction (TD), and normal direction (ND). Panel (b) displays 2D diffraction intensity maps as functions of azimuthal angle (0°–360°) and 2-Theta angle (5°–12°), comparing non-deformed, ~30% strained-RD, and ~30% strained-TD conditions, revealing distinct diffraction rings for FCC austenite (γ) and BCC ferrite/martensite (α) phases. Panel (c) shows integrated 1D diffraction profiles with experimental data (open symbols) and Rietveld refinement calculations (solid lines), identifying five FCC reflections ({111}, {200}, {220}, {311}, {222}) and four BCC reflections ({110}, {200}, {211}, {220}), demonstrating good agreement between experiment and simulation for phase quantification. Panel (d) presents single peak fitting results using Gaussian functions for the sector along RD (azimuth angle -5° to 5°), deconvoluting contributions from FCC (green) and BCC (blue) phases to determine interplanar spacing and lattice strain. This figure establishes the methodological foundation for quantitative analysis of strain-induced martensitic transformation, lattice strain evolution, and phase-specific deformation behavior under different loading orientations.

This figure presents comprehensive EBSD characterization results of the non-deformed low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) after intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 min. Subfigure (a) shows a 3D reconstruction of Phase + IQ mapping from three orthogonal planes (RD, TD, ND), revealing a distinct banded/layered microstructure aligned along the rolling direction (RD) with BCC phases (δ+α ferrite, 61.2 vol.%, white) and FCC austenite (38.8 vol.%, cyan) distributed in alternating layers at the 100 μm scale. Subfigure (b) provides a magnified RD-TD plane view (5 μm scale) clearly identifying three phases: coarse δ-ferrite, ultrafine α-ferrite, and γ-austenite, where the uniform IQ contrast in BCC phases confirms the absence of martensite in the as-annealed condition. Subfigure (c) displays the corresponding 3D inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation mapping, illustrating the crystallographic texture of the banded structure. Subfigures (d) and (e) quantify the grain size distributions: austenite exhibits a fine average grain size of ~1.8 μm, α-ferrite ~2 μm, while δ-ferrite shows a much coarser average size of ~38 μm. The microstructural architecture consists of 25 vol.% coarse δ-ferrite (retained from hot rolling) embedded within 56 vol.% ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates (formed via reverse transformation from martensite during annealing), providing the structural basis for the subsequent anisotropic deformation behavior and strain partitioning between soft δ-ferrite and hard UFG γ+α regions.

This figure presents the anisotropic tensile mechanical behavior of a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) processed by hot rolling, cold rolling, and intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 minutes. Subfigure (a) displays the engineering stress-strain curves comparing the rolling direction (RD, red curve) and transverse direction (TD, blue curve), where the RD sample exhibits superior ultimate tensile strength (~864 MPa) and ductility (69% total elongation) compared to the TD sample. Subfigure (b) illustrates the corresponding strain hardening rate curves (upper curves) and true stress-strain curves (lower curves), revealing a three-stage strain hardening behavior: Stage I features a sharp drop in hardening rate, Stage II shows an increasing hardening rate starting from ~0.2 true strain, and Stage III exhibits a decreasing trend until necking. The RD sample demonstrates a higher maximum strain hardening rate (2052 MPa) than the TD sample (1828 MPa), which caption states is attributed to faster strain-induced α'-martensite transformation kinetics in the RD orientation due to different strain partitioning between the banded δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates.

This figure presents comprehensive EBSD characterization of the microstructural evolution in a low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) subjected to 15% engineering strain along the transverse direction (TD). Panel (a) displays the phase + band slope (BS) map, distinguishing FCC austenite (green) from BCC phases (red, δ/α-ferrite and α′-martensite) with BS values ranging from 20 to 255. Panel (b) shows a bimodal BS distribution for the BCC phase, where the low-BS peak (~100) corresponds to deformation-induced α′-martensite with high dislocation density, while the high-BS peak (~180) represents ferrite. The kernel average misorientation (KAM) map in (c) reveals higher local misorientation in the ultrafine-grained γ+α region compared to δ-ferrite. The magnified phase map in (d) identifies γ austenite (green), α/δ-ferrite (red), and α′-martensite (gray), demonstrating that martensite nucleates preferentially at phase boundaries between austenite and ferrite (marked by arrows). Panel (e) presents the KAM map for the FCC phase alone, highlighting elevated misorientation (yellow/red regions indicated by arrows) at heterointerfaces, evidencing strain concentration and geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) accumulation at these boundaries. These results collectively support that the TRIP effect occurs primarily at phase boundaries due to local stress/strain partitioning.

This figure presents TEM micrographs showing austenite deformation mechanisms in a Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al (wt.%) low-density medium manganese steel TD (transverse direction) sample subjected to 15% engineering strain. The figure comprises six panels: (a1) bright-field (BF) and (a2) dark-field (DF) image pairs revealing strain-induced α'-martensite nucleation at the γ/α (austenite/ferrite) phase boundary, with corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirming the FCC (Z=[011]) structure of austenite and BCC (Z=[-111]) structure of martensite; and (b1) BF and (b2) DF image pairs showing α'-martensite formation at the γ/δ (austenite/δ-ferrite) interface, accompanied by equivalent diffraction patterns. The dark-field images highlight the α'-martensite variants through specific reflection imaging. These micrographs provide direct evidence that strain-induced martensitic transformation preferentially nucleates at heterophase boundaries (γ/α and γ/δ), where strain incompatibility leads to accumulation of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) and high local stress concentrations. This observation supports the transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) mechanism contributing to the anisotropic deformation behavior, demonstrating that phase boundaries serve as critical nucleation sites for α'-martensite during tensile deformation.

This figure presents comprehensive TEM characterization of deformation substructures in a low-density medium manganese steel (TD sample, 15% engineering strain). Subfigures (a1) and (a2) display bright-field and high-resolution TEM images of austenite (γ, FCC, Z=[011]), revealing dense stacking faults (SFs) marked by red arrows and planar dislocation slip traces at scales of 500 nm and 2 nm respectively. Subfigures (b1) and (b2) show δ-ferrite (BCC, Z=[-111]) containing high-density dislocation walls and dislocation tangles (indicated by arrows) imaged under two-beam condition with g=110, with scales of 2 μm and 200 nm. Subfigures (c1) and (c2) illustrate α-ferrite (BCC) exhibiting similar dislocation tangles under g=110 diffraction condition at 200 nm and 100 nm scales. The contrasting deformation mechanisms—planar slip with stacking faults in the low-SFE austenite versus cross-slip induced dislocation tangles in the BCC ferrite phases—demonstrate the critical role of stacking fault energy in determining strain partitioning and transformation-induced plasticity behavior.

Figure 8 presents in-situ high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) analysis revealing the anisotropic deformation behavior of a low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) processed by hot/cold rolling and intercritical annealing at 850°C for 10 min. Panel (a) displays the evolution of diffraction peaks at true strains of 0.14, 0.22, 0.3, and 0.38 for both rolling direction (RD, red curves) and transverse direction (TD, blue curves) samples. The FCC peaks (γ311, γ222, marked by orange diamonds) corresponding to austenite decrease in intensity with increasing strain, while BCC/BCT peaks (α211, α220, marked by green circles) corresponding to ferrite/martensite increase, indicating strain-induced martensitic transformation. Notably, BCC peak intensities are higher in RD samples than TD samples at high strains (ε>0.22), suggesting enhanced martensite formation in RD. Panel (b) quantifies the martensite fraction calculated by the Rietveld refinement method as a function of true strain, showing that RD exhibits faster transformation kinetics with a difference of up to 4 vol.% at ε≈0.48 compared to TD. The inset displays the corresponding strain hardening rate curves, confirming higher work hardening capability in RD. These results demonstrate that anisotropic strain partitioning between banded δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates leads to different austenite strain levels, governing the TRIP effect and mechanical anisotropy.

Figure 9 presents the evolution of dislocation density in austenite for a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al) tested along rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD). Panel (a) shows the modified Williamson-Hall plots of $\Delta K - \beta W_{hkl}$ versus $KC^{1/2}$ for five FCC crystallographic reflections ({111}, {200}, {220}, {311}, {222}) at true strains of 0.14 and 0.22. The RD samples (red lines/markers) exhibit consistently higher slopes than TD samples (blue lines/markers), indicating higher dislocation densities. Panel (b) quantifies the dislocation density evolution as a function of true strain, revealing that RD samples maintain higher dislocation density throughout deformation, with a maximum difference of approximately $1.51 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{-2}$ compared to TD samples. This anisotropic dislocation multiplication is attributed to strain-induced $\alpha'$-martensitic transformation and associated strain partitioning between phases, where the volume expansion during transformation generates localized plasticity and enhanced dislocation activity in the austenite matrix adjacent to martensite.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of strain partitioning behavior in a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) loaded along the rolling direction (RD). The microstructure consists of banded coarse-grained δ-ferrite (25 vol.%, ~38 μm) and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates (56 vol.% total, with γ ~1.8 μm and α ~2 μm). Panels (a₁)-(a₅) show the entire probed area: (a₁) is the EBSD phase + band contrast map distinguishing δ-ferrite from UFG regions, while (a₂)-(a₅) display shear strain distributions at engineering strains of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%, respectively, revealing progressive strain localization. Panels (b₁)-(b₅) focus on the UFG γ+α region marked by the red rectangle in (a₁), with (b₁) showing the phase map and (b₂)-(b₅) showing corresponding strain maps at increasing deformation levels. Single-phase maps for FCC (γ-austenite) and BCC (δ and α-ferrite) phases are presented in (c₁)-(c₅) and (d₁)-(d₅), respectively, demonstrating heterogeneous strain accumulation within each phase. Panels (e₁) and (e₂) quantify local shear strain profiles across phase boundaries (along arrows in insets), showing strain jumps at δ/γ+α interfaces. The color scale indicates shear strain from blue (low, ~8%) to red (high, ~20%). The data supports that while δ-ferrite and UFG aggregates deform under near iso-strain conditions in the RD sample, significant strain partitioning occurs within the UFG region where γ-austenite accommodates higher strain than α-ferrite, promoting strain-induced martensitic transformation (TRIP effect) preferentially at phase boundaries.

This figure illustrates the strain partitioning behavior in the transverse direction (TD) sample of a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%), investigated using electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) coupled with high-resolution digital image correlation (DIC). The upper panels display EBSD phase and band contrast maps showing a banded microstructure consisting of coarse-grained δ-ferrite (BCC, cyan) and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates (white/light gray), alongside single-phase maps for FCC (austenite) and BCC (ferrite) phases at the 5 μm scale. The central panels present shear strain distribution maps at engineering strains of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%, revealing progressive strain localization with increasing deformation. The lower graphs provide quantitative line profiles of shear strain across representative phase regions, demonstrating that the TD sample exhibits pronounced strain heterogeneity with higher strain concentrated in the δ-ferrite phase (up to ~30% shear strain) compared to the UFG γ+α region, contrasting with the more uniform strain distribution observed in the rolling direction (RD) sample.

This figure comprises four line graphs quantifying the average shear strain evolution as a function of engineering strain (5–20%) for different microstructural constituents in a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al) processed by intercritical annealing. Based on high-resolution micro-digital image correlation (μ-DIC), subfigures (a) and (b) [as labeled in the figure, corresponding to RD and TD respectively in the caption context] depict the strain partitioning between coarse-grained δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates. In the RD sample, both phases exhibit nearly identical shear strain values increasing linearly to ~15%, indicating homogeneous strain distribution under an iso-strain condition. Conversely, in the TD sample, δ-ferrite shows significantly higher shear strain (~15% vs ~12% at 20% engineering strain) than the γ+α aggregates, revealing strain concentration in the softer δ-phase. Subfigures (c) and (d) [as labeled] further resolve the strain distribution within the UFG γ+α regions, comparing the γ-α' mixed phase (austenite plus martensite) against the α-ferrite. For both orientations, the γ-α' mixed phase accumulates higher shear strain (up to ~16.5% in RD and ~14.5% in TD at 20% strain) compared to the α phase (~13% and ~11.5% respectively), with the RD sample showing approximately 1.15 times higher strain in austenite than the TD sample. These observations demonstrate that loading-direction-dependent strain partitioning dictates the plastic strain concentration in austenite, thereby governing the kinetics of strain-induced martensitic transformation and the resultant strain-hardening behavior.

This figure presents high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) measurements of lattice strain evolution during tensile deformation of a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%), comparing rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD) samples. Subfigures (a) and (b) display lattice strains of individual crystallographic planes—five FCC (austenite) reflections (111, 200, 220, 311, 222) and four BCC (ferrite) reflections (110, 200, 211, 220)—as functions of applied engineering strain, revealing elastic anisotropy and distinct yielding behaviors between phases. Subfigures (c) and (d) show weighted average lattice strains for FCC and BCC phases, with arrows indicating deviation from linearity marking the onset of plastic deformation. In RD samples, austenite yields at 1.8% strain while ferrite yields at 2.3%; in TD samples, these values are 2.1% and 2.9%, respectively. The earlier yielding of austenite demonstrates it is the softer phase, and the directional differences in yielding strains confirm anisotropic deformation behavior related to the banded microstructure of δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained γ+α aggregates.

This figure presents the deformation-induced martensitic transformation kinetics in a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) during tensile deformation along two perpendicular directions. The plot shows martensite fraction (vol.%) as a function of true strain (0–0.5), comparing experimental data from synchrotron high-energy X-ray diffraction (HEXRD) with predictions from the Olson-Cohen (OC) model. Three curves are displayed: Exp.RD (red circles) for the rolling direction, Exp.TD (blue circles) for the transverse direction, and Model (dashed black lines) representing fitted curves. The RD sample exhibits significantly faster transformation kinetics, reaching ~25% martensite at 0.5 strain compared to ~20% in the TD sample. The OC model (with parameters n=2 and initial austenite fraction of 46.5 vol.%) accurately captures both datasets by incorporating a strain partitioning factor of 1.15 (ε_RD = 1.15 ε_TD), demonstrating that loading direction-dependent strain distribution governs the anisotropic transformation behavior.

The figure presents microscale digital image correlation (μ-DIC) maps showing the spatial distribution of local shear strain across the phase boundary between coarse-grained δ-ferrite and ultrafine-grained (UFG) γ+α aggregates in a low-density medium manganese steel (Fe-7.95Mn-0.25C-4.94Al wt.%) subjected to tensile loading along the rolling direction (RD). Panels (a) and (b) display two representative regions captured at increasing macroscopic strain levels of 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. The color scale ranges from blue (0% shear strain) to red (30% shear strain), where dashed black lines delineate the phase boundaries and red arrows highlight strain concentration sites. As macroscopic strain increases from 5% to 20%, pronounced strain localization (evident as yellow-to-red regions) develops progressively at the δ-ferrite/γ+α interfaces, indicating severe strain partitioning and incompatibility between the soft δ-ferrite and hard UFG aggregates. These observations provide direct evidence that the iso-strain condition in the RD sample generates high geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) densities and stress concentrations at phase boundaries, which promotes martensitic transformation kinetics.
